data.title: GPIQ: 10% Yield Plus Growth Potential
data.description:
The Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF (NASDAQ:GPIQ) is a tech-oriented covered call ETF that is focusing on the generation of consistent monthly distributions and long term net asset value growth. It can make strategic sense for investors to add covered call ETFs to their portfolios because they allow investors to monetize a core long position in a portfolio. With regard to the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF, the fund's portfolio consists of the world’s leading technology companies including Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), Tesla (TSLA) and other magnificent 7 stocks.

Covered call ETF with a bent towards technology/magnificent 7 stocks

The Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF is a covered call ETF with a focus on the Nasdaq stocks and therefore has a considerable overweighting of technology and magnificent 7 companies. The GPIQ is an ETF of the lower market cap category, managing just a bit more than a billion dollars in assets and it therefore considerably trails the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ), a major rival ETF, which has a 25X higher investment volume.

I recommended the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium ETF (and have a long position in the ETF) due to the fact that it achieved double-digit NAV returns since inception. As we will learn later, the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF out-performed many of its covered call ETF rivals in terms of net asset value performance since inception and is therefore a top choice for investors that want to generate income.

While GPIQ has a much lower asset base than the JEPQ, even smaller covered call ETFs can be promising for income-oriented investors, especially those investors that have a favorable view on artificial intelligence integration, AI adoption and CapEx spending in the hyperscale market.

What I like specifically about the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF is that the ETF is overweighting Nvidia and magnificent 7 stocks that have the strongest growth prospects in the market. According to current long EPS growth estimates for magnificent 7 companies, Nvidia is expected to have by far the largest earnings growth (+29% annually) in the years ahead.

Nvidia is the largest ETF holding with a weighting of 9.9%, followed by Microsoft (MSFT), weighed at 9%, and Apple (AAPL), weighed at 7.4%. Nvidia just reached a record-topping four trillion dollar market valuation as well on the back of some positive news about its H20 chips.

Magnificent 7 companies represent 40.9% of the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF while its top ten holdings represent 51.7% of its total assets. This means that the ETF is highly concentrated in a small number of stocks -- the magnificent 7 which include Nvidia, Alphabet, Microsoft, Apple, Meta Platforms, Tesla and Amazon.

GPIQ's high concentration in its top ten holdings and in magnificent 7 stocks, however, is not unusual for a tech-focused covered call ETF, and it makes the ETF especially interesting for investors that believe technology stocks have out-performance potential going forward, mainly due to accelerating AI spending in Data Centers.

The GPIQ, in terms of top ten holdings and magnificent 7 concentration, is about average, but has a more aggressive investment posture than the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF.

% of Fund Assets GPIQ JEPQ QYLD QQQI Average
Top Ten Holdings 51.7% 41.7% 53.7% 49.6% 49.2%
Mag 7 Holdings 40.9% 34.6% 42.3% 38.9% 39.2%

(Source: Author)

Key NAV growth drivers

I personally have a highly favorable view on technology companies as I expect CapEx spending in the Data Center industry to seriously ramp up in the years ahead. Here, according to projections from Dell, Data Center spending is expected to grow by a factor of 4X between 2023 and 2028, to a size of $1.0 trillion. This growth is chiefly driven by accelerating adoption and integration of AI and could represent a massive, multi-year revenue growth opportunity, not just for Nvidia, but also for other magnificent 7 stocks that have considerable exposure to the AI theme in their core businesses.

Because of a massive expected expansion in the total addressable market and surging AI-related investment opportunities, companies and hyperscalers are expected to see a massive up-swing in revenues, free cash flows and earnings as a result. Nvidia, as an example, just received a major catalyst relating to the resumption of H20 GPU shipments to China... which were blocked in Q1'25 by the U.S. government limited chip exports. The resumption of H20 shipments could add ~$15B in revenue to Nvidia's top line just this year and up to $30B next year, and could therefore set this specific magnificent 7 stock up for a growth acceleration.

GPIQ’s net asset value performance

The Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF is a fairly new ETF issue and has been incepted only in October 2023. Since then, however, the covered call ETF has achieved an impressive average annualized net asset value return of 25%.

GPIQ’s valuation

The Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF is growing its net asset value and could be an attractive addition to an income-oriented investment portfolio with a bent towards technology stocks, especially in a magnificent 7 category.

The Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF, despite being much smaller than the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF, has out-performed its bigger covered call ETF rival by a considerable margin, in terms of NAV performance, since its inception in 2023. The GPIQ also out-performed other covered call ETFs like the NEO Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (NEOS) -- which I recommended highly -- as well as the Global X Nasdaq 100 Covered Call ETF (QYLD)... which is one of the longest running covered call ETFs in the market.

In terms of valuation, ETFs are valued at net asset value as their portfolios consist of marketable securities of highly liquid, large-cap U.S. technology companies. Currently, shares of the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF are valued at a moderate 0.06% premium to net asset value, making it only slightly more expensive than the JPMorgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF.

Risks with GPIQ

Sector-specific ETFs can do very well for investors if the tech sector remains in play and in favor with investors. On the other hand, if a sector like tech rotates out-of-favor with investors, the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF has potential to under-perform even a broadly diversified index such as the S&P 500. The high concentration of magnificent 7 stocks is also something that may not appeal to all (covered call ETF) investors. In this case, a more broadly diversified covered call ETF like the NEOS S&P 500 High ETF may be an investment alternative.

Final thoughts

The Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF is a potentially attractive buy for investors that want to gain long term net asset value upside related to the world’s fastest-growing tech franchises while generating recurring income from a covered call/option overlay strategy. In my opinion, the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF has done well in its relatively short history and with a 10% yield on offer, I believe the Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Core Premium Income ETF is a rock-solid covered covered ETF to buy for both income and long term NAV growth.

data.title: PepsiCo Q2: Scoop Up This Dividend All-Star Now
data.description:
As a kid and a young adult, I have always enjoyed watching basketball. College basketball and the NBA. The game fascinates me.

In a sense, investing and basketball aren't all that different.

Some players are more well-versed on offense. Others are more well-suited on defense. It's not too common that a player can dominate on both ends of the court.

I view my investment portfolio as a team. I like to pair offensive stocks (think growth stocks) with defensive stocks (think value stocks). That's because I find that this provides me with a nice blend of income potential (my portfolio yields around 3%) and long-term capital appreciation.

NVIDIA (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), and Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) are my three biggest holdings, making up nearly 18% of the portfolio. This gives me plenty of firepower in a bull market.

I balance the volatility with my next three largest holdings, Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Realty Income (O), and British American Tobacco (BTI). These make up another 13% of the portfolio.

Further down the list of my value stocks is the focus of today, PepsiCo (NASDAQ:PEP). When I last covered it with a buy rating in March, I thought that PepsiCo had levers that it could pull to deliver future growth. The A-rated balance sheet was another plus. Shares were also trading at a 15% discount to my fair value estimate.

After the 7% earnings pop following the release of Q2 earnings earlier today, I'm reaffirming my buy rating. PepsiCo comfortably delivered a double beat and reiterated its guidance for 2025. The company outlined plans to relaunch Lay's and Tostitos, grow its away-from-home channel, and improve operating efficiency. In the years to come, I believe these moves will be positive. PepsiCo remains financially strong, too. Finally, the stock looks to still be favorably valued for the long run.

Don't Count PepsiCo Out

On July 17th, PepsiCo reported its second quarter results for the period ended June 14th, 2025. The company's net revenue grew by 1% over the year-ago period to $22.73 billion in the quarter. This managed to surpass analyst consensus by $430 million during the quarter.

What was behind this net revenue beat for the second quarter? Organic net revenue logged a 2.1% year-over-year growth rate in the quarter. PepsiCo's effective net pricing actions helped the topline by approximately 4% during the quarter.

Consumers only partially pushed back against these price increases, with volume falling 1.5% over the year-ago period for the second quarter. The other headwind in the quarter was unfavorable foreign currency translation to the tune of 1.5%. The company attributed this to declines in the Mexican peso, Turkish lira, and Brazilian real.

PepsiCo's EPS dropped 59.1% year-over-year to $0.92 during the second quarter. This was the result of $1.86 billion in impairment charges tied to the Rockstar and Be & Cheery brands. Backing this out, the company's core constant currency EPS decreased by 5% over the year-ago period to $2.12 for the quarter. This topped the analyst consensus in the quarter by $0.09.

Beyond the near-term challenges that I attribute to a post-COVID-19 pandemic normalization, I believe that PepsiCo can return to its former glory. This is going to be done in a few different ways.

For one, PepsiCo is working hard to improve its brand image. Aside from the Siete acquisition that I discussed in the past, another way that the company is elevating brand perception is through strategic brand relaunches.

In his remarks during the Q2 2025 Earnings Call, Chairman and CEO Ramon Laguarta pointed to an ongoing relaunch of Lay's and Tostitos to build on its existing Simply line. These will relaunch in Q4 2025 and Q1 2026 without artificial colors or flavors.

Laguarta also discussed the company's focus on its away-from-home channel. As consumers are consuming more calories away from home, PepsiCo has added innovation via mini-meals and ready-to-eat solutions. As the company builds momentum in its away-from-home channel, margins will likely expand further for PepsiCo. That's because Lagaurta noted that these channels are margin accretive for both beverages and foods.

One final catalyst for PepsiCo is the integration of its two North American businesses. Given the recent investments that the company has made in technology and data, it has a more comprehensive system in place. This will help PepsiCo to integrate its value chain and opportunistically reduce its footprint over the next few years.

In the near term, CFO James Caulfield expects 70% more productivity in the second half than was delivered in the first half. Along with foreign exchange headwinds recently moderating from the weakening U.S. dollar, this is why PepsiCo expects core constant currency EPS to be flat in 2025.

Beyond this year, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for 5.8% core constant currency EPS growth in 2026. Another 4.6% growth in core constant currency EPS is anticipated for 2027.

PepsiCo is also financially robust. Adjusting for the impairment charge, the company's interest coverage ratio would have been 11.9 in the first half of 2025. That's part of how the business earns an A+ credit rating from S&P with a stable outlook (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to PepsiCo's Q2 2025 Earnings Press Release and PepsiCo's Q2 2025 10-Q Filing).

The Stock Has Compelling Upside Potential

In the four months since I last covered PepsiCo, shares have posted a -1% total return. This has meaningfully lagged the S&P 500 index's (SP500) 13% total return over that time.

Not surprisingly, I still believe that PepsiCo is a solid value. Shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 17.3. For more color, that's materially less than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 23, per FAST Graphs.

After PepsiCo's decent earnings beat, I'm confident the analyst consensus for core constant currency EPS will rise. I project that the consensus will increase from $7.90 for the 2025 pre-Q2 earnings report to $8.15 (essentially flat versus 2024 and in line with company guidance). This also leads me to believe that the consensus for 2026 will edge higher from $8.35 to $8.50.

Assuming flat core constant currency EPS for 2025, PepsiCo could generate 5% to 6% annual growth in 2026 and beyond. The company's 10-year annual growth rate of 5.8% falls within this range. That implies that putting a year of normalization in 2025 after several years of growth coming out of the COVID-19 pandemic, PepsiCo's growth story remains on track.

Of course, the lower 10-year U.S. Treasury yields (US10Y) that supported the stock's valuation multiple in the past probably won't be coming back. In the past 10 years, the average 10-year yield was around 2.5%, according to data from Macrotrends. I continue to expect that the 10-year yield will average the mid to high 3% range in the years ahead.

Historically, PepsiCo's earnings yield has traded at an approximately 175 basis point investment spread to the 10-year yield. In recent years, this has narrowed to between 125 basis points and 150 basis points, however.

Applying a 125 basis point spread to a 10-year yield in the high 3% range, I get a fair value P/E ratio of 20. That would be three turns below the 10-year average P/E ratio, or a bit more than one standard deviation.

As of July 17th, 2025, the calendar year 2025 is 54% complete. This suggests another 46% of 2025 and 54% of 2026 remain ahead over the coming 12 months. That produces a forward 12-month core constant currency EPS input of $8.34.

Using my fair value P/E ratio of 20, I compute a fair value of $167 a share. Against the current $144 share price, this works out to be a 14% discount to fair value. If PepsiCo meets growth expectations and returns to my fair value P/E ratio, it could have an 18% upside through June 2026 (adjusting for the rally today). By the end of 2030, the stock could generate at least 10% annual total returns.

More Dividend Growth To Come

PepsiCo's 4% forward dividend yield is about 100 basis points above the consumer staples sector median forward yield of 3%. This gap has grown by about 10 basis points since I last covered the stock. As a result, Seeking Alpha's Quant System has issued respective A- and B grades for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield (up from B+ and B- previously).

As if this superior starting yield wasn't enough, there's more about PepsiCo to like. The company has hiked its dividends paid to shareholders for 53 consecutive years, cementing its status as a Dividend King. For more context, that's substantially better than the sector median of just 2.8 years. That's how PepsiCo earns an A+ grade from the Quant System for overall dividend consistency.

The company also has a clear path to build on its dividend growth for the foreseeable future. This is because PepsiCo's core constant currency EPS payout ratio is set to be in the high 60% range for 2025. According to the Zen Research Terminal, this remains slightly better than the 70% payout ratio that rating agencies prefer for the industry.

PepsiCo's free cash flow payout ratio will be tight in 2025 at around 95%. However, an anticipated surge in FCF per share in 2026 is expected to return this to a much healthier level in the high 70% range. That gives PepsiCo the capital that it needs to not only pay a growing dividend but also fund bolt-on acquisitions and share buybacks. This explains why the Quant System awards a B grade for overall dividend safety.

As PepsiCo returns to its typical growth, dividend growth should also slightly improve versus the most recent 5.1% dividend raise. The Quant System anticipates a forward annual dividend per share growth rate of 6%, which I believe is reasonable. That would be above the sector median of 4.9%. This is enough to warrant a B- grade from the Quant System for the forward dividend per share growth metric.

Risks To Consider

PepsiCo is a world-class business that I believe can and will rebound. Yet, it does carry risks that are worth pointing out.

The first risk to PepsiCo is that its free cash flow per share may not rebound as quickly or to the extent that analysts anticipate. If FCF per share is slow to recover, this could weigh on the company's future dividend growth. That could continue to weigh on sentiment toward the stock.

PepsiCo also faces the long-term risk that the GLP-1 drug class could become more prevalent. This could put pressure on the company's volumes in the future. As I pointed out in March, a Cornell University and Numerator study revealed that the drug class reduced grocery spending by 6% within six months of starting the medication. The sharpest drop observed (-11%) was in processed foods like chips and cookies.

Another risk to PepsiCo is the possibility that it can't improve its brand perception among consumers with its latest batch of product relaunches. An inability to shed the "junk food" label that many consumers have toward its products could make it more difficult to reinvigorate its growth prospects.

One last risk to the company is the potential for long-term interest rates to remain above my expectations. That could prevent PepsiCo from returning to a meaningfully higher valuation multiple, which could limit future upside.

Summary: A SWAN Cornerstone Of My Portfolio

As my 14th-biggest holding, PepsiCo remains a top holding for me. It comprises 1.9% of my total portfolio value and contributes 2.4% of my total dividend income.

I believe PepsiCo's product relaunches and stepped-up productivity will soon bring growth back. I also like the company for its A+ S&P credit rating and low cost of capital. PepsiCo's dividend remains set up for more mid-single-digit percentage growth. Shares are also undervalued enough that I believe 10% annual total returns over the medium term are likely. For these reasons, I'm standing by my buy rating.

data.title: Aeva Technologies: Differentiated LiDAR Developer With A Demanding Valuation
data.description:
Aeva Technologies (NASDAQ:AEVA) is a developer of FMCW LiDAR technology. While Aeva is targeting automotive applications, it is differentiated by its focus on using LiDAR as a displacement sensor for manufacturing automation. This part of the business is still nascent, but early traction appears promising.

Aeva's share price has sky rocketed in recent months, as the commercial potential of its products has become more apparent. Given the current state of the business, Aeva's market capitalization is now extremely high, making an investment at this point in time speculative. This is illustrated by the high volatility in Aeva's share price in recent weeks.

Market

FMCW (Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave) LiDAR uses the Doppler effect to simultaneously determine position and velocity. In comparison, normal LiDAR must infer velocity from multiple measurements of position. FMCW also has lower power requirements and is free from interference from other LiDAR sensors. While these properties could enable Aeva to target a range of end markets, the company is focused on automotive, consumer devices, industrial automation and security.

The automotive opportunity is the largest at the moment, with growth likely to accelerate in coming years as robotaxis proliferate and more sophisticated ADAS systems are introduced in consumer vehicles. Luminar estimates that the ADAS and autonomous driving technology TAM will grow from less than $5 billion currently to $150 billion or more in 2030. More than 100 million new passenger vehicles are expected to be manufactured annually through 2030 and beyond, and Luminar anticipates roughly a 4% vehicle penetration rate across the industry.

There are also significant opportunities in adjacent markets, with much of Aeva's current focus on the use of LiDAR as a displacement sensor in industrial applications. The displacement sensor market is a $4 billion plus opportunity with annual volumes in excess of 2 million sensors. This market is expected to experience solid growth in coming years, supported in part by the adoption of laser displacement sensors. Factory and process automation applications include:

  • Positioning and guidance for volume manufacturing
  • Component inspection for electronics manufacturing
  • Automotive parts inspection
  • Packaging parts measurement

Aeva’s FMCW technology offers greater precision and stand-off flexibility in a smaller and lower cost form factor. The ability to directly measure velocity could also open up new applications.

Aeva

Aeva has developed a fully solid-state FMCW LiDAR which integrates silicon photonics technology. Aeva's LiDAR breaks the dependency between maximum range and point density, providing high resolution at long distances (up to 500 meters range). This is a fiberless solution that is based on scalable semiconductor manufacturing processes. A custom digital processing ASIC reduces the number of transceivers required to provide high resolution performance across the entire field of view. In addition, Aeva's detector does not use any exotic materials, helping to limit cost. It also has significantly lower power requirements than other approaches and is available in a compact form factor.

Aeva's products include:

  • Aeries II - LiDAR targeted at industrial and automotive applications. Aeries II provides over 120º field of view for distances up to 500 meters. Velocity measurement with centimeter per second precision.
  • Atlas - high-performance FMCW designed for mass production. Offers long range and is targeted at automotive applications.
  • Atlas Ultra - designed to meet the performance demands of Level 3-4 ADAS. Ultra provides up to 3x higher resolution and a field of view of up to 150 degrees.

Aeva is engaged with customers regarding integration into passenger vehicles, trucks and robotaxis. This includes a truck production program with Daimler. Daimler and Torc Robotics selected Aeva's LiDARs for incorporation into their autonomous trucks, with production expected to start by 2026, and volumes expected to ramp in 2027. Aeva also believes that it is on track with a global top 10 automotive OEM. A development program with this customer is underway and a large scale production program is expected to be awarded later in the year. In addition, Aeva is supplying LiDARs to Inyo Mobility for its autonomous shuttle program. Inyo’s shuttles are designed for last mile urban public transportation.

Perhaps more importantly in the near-term, Aeva also offers Eve 1D, an FMCW high-precision laser displacement sensor for industrial automation use cases. Aeva's LiDAR provides sub micron precision at a low cost and in a compact unit. Due to its coaxial measurement, Eve can operate across a wide range of target standoff distances (up to 200 meters). Eve also enables faster and inspection, supporting higher throughput.

Eve 1 has already started to achieve commercial traction, with SICK and LMI Technologies recently signing on as the first customers. These companies are leaders in precision sensing solutions, with more than 15% market share, representing approximately 300,000 sensors annually. Aeva is also engaged with other companies. Over 1,000 sensors have been ordered, and are expected to be shipped later this year. Aeva is hoping for tens of thousands of units in the near-term, with an expansion to 100,000 plus units longer term. This could be a significant business given that ASPs are in the thousands of dollars.

Aeva is also expanding into ITS (Intelligent Transportation Systems) through its relationship with Sensys Gatso. Sensys Gatso is a global leader in traffic safety enforcement solutions, with more than 60,000 deployments across 60 countries. Aeva will be the exclusive LiDAR supplier for a mobile speed detection product in Australia and deployment of these sensors has already begun. Aeva is also working exploring with Sensys to explore the rollout of its sensors in additional markets and there is reportedly interest from other leaders in the ITS market.

Another part of Aeva's business that could help to drive growth is its strategic collaboration with a Global Fortune 500 company’s tech subsidiary. The Fortune 500 company plans on investing up to $50 million in Aeva and will help commercialize Aeva's sensors across end markets like consumer electronics, industrial and automotive. As part of this, the partner will take approximately a 6% stake of equity in Aeva. Aeva will also use this company as its Tier 2 manufacturing partner for its auto program.

Financial Analysis

Aeva generated $3.4 million revenue in the first quarter. Most revenue currently comes from product protypes and non-recurring engineering services, including from the Daimler Truck program. Revenue growth this year is expected to be in the 80-100% range compared to 2024, excluding the impact of the recently announced strategic collaboration.

Aeva is still in the process of establishing its business though, and as a result, its current margins have little meaning. For example, Aeva's gross margins are still negative at the moment. This should change over the next few years though.

Aeva's non-GAAP operating loss was $25.9 million in the first quarter. Operating cash burn was approximately $31 million, while CapEx totaled $0.5 million. Cash usage is generally elevated in the first quarter though due to working capital changes.

Losses seem set to moderate going forward though given expected revenue growth and the fact that Aeva wants to reduce its non-GAAP operating expenses by 10-20% in 2025.

Aeva ended the first quarter with $206 million available liquidity, with $125 million of this related to an undrawn credit facility. This figure will soon be boosted by roughly $32.5 million due to the Fortune 500 company's equity investment. This likely provides Aeva with at least a 2 year cash runway. Aeva's $1 billion plus market capitalization also means that additional capital can be raised without excessively diluting existing investors.

Conclusion

Most of Aeva's revenue currently comes from non-recurring engineering, which is fairly meaningless. While revenue is expected to ramp in 2026 / 2027, near-term revenue expectations are modest versus Aeva's market capitalization (~$1.5 billion). Aeva's growth is expected to be robust in coming years, although analyst revenue projections have plummeted in recent years.

I don't have high expectations for Aeva's automotive business, particularly prior to 2027. While Aeva has a strong solution from a technology perspective, imaging radar is likely to undermine the importance of FMCW LiDAR in automotive applications. Aeva's relationship with Daimler is probably its most concrete near-term automotive opportunity, with the company planning on increasing its production capacity to 200,000 units annually, in part to support Daimler / Torc Robotics.

The industrial automation opportunity is probably the most important driver of Aeva's share price at the moment, although this market isn't overly large. While Eve appears promising, automotive success will probably be needed to drive strong returns.

data.title: Gamestop: No More Memes, But The Trading Card Age Is Just Beginning (Surprising Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Perhaps there is no stock other than Gamestop (NYSE:GME) that is most well known as a “meme stock.” Yet I’d argue that this latest bull market has created a new set of meme stocks with GME somehow being left out. There’s some irony in that, however, with GME having a refreshed balance sheet and leaned out cost structure that has more or less fully removed any imminent threat of bankruptcy. Yet the most surprising and overlooked development is instead the company’s pivot into the trading card market, which I suspect many have discounted as being irrelevant. I view this transition to be anything but, and enough reason to upgrade the stock to a buy rating.

GME Stock Price

I last covered GME in February 2021 where I detailed an options trade to earn a 100% annualized yield. That trade worked out great, though GME has underperformed the broader market by triple digits since then.

Today I return to the name with a surprising conclusion: there’s a real fundamental story here and the market is sleeping.

GME Stock Key Metrics

GME has come a long way. It was previously written off as a struggling brick and mortar retailer. The company took advantage of meme mania to pay down debt and build up an impressive cash position, all while also gradually exiting unprofitable locations. These efforts have yielded great dividends, with the company somehow posting profitable results even as revenues decline.

I note that the profitability gains are no fluke, as the company was able to report just a $26.2 million GAAP operating loss and $131.3 million GAAP net profit in the 2024 full year.

The main driver of the profitability is the significant interest income generated from $6.4 billion of cash (the company has since spent $500 million to purchase Bitcoin) versus $1.5 billion of debt.

The balance sheet is a big driver of the bullish thesis and is often overlooked by bears. But today I want to bring greater focus on the booming collectibles business, which saw revenues jump over 50% in the most recent quarter.

Is GME Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

GME has long sold a variety of collectibles but it was only late last year that the company made an aggressive push into selling trading cards. This also includes allowing customers to submit their cards for “grading.” For those unfamiliar with the trading card market, this might seem like a big nothingburger, but luckily (or unluckily) yours truly happens to be quite fond of trading cards.

I am of the view that this transition to the trading card market is bullish - even highly bullish - for the direction of the company. Without mincing words, I view the trading card market (which typically involves purchasing packs of cards with the potential but no guarantee of receiving something valuable) as one of the last forms of “unregulated gambling.” Prior to GME’s entrance, the typical consumer might purchase these cards both online or in-person at somewhere like Target (TGT). I emphasize that e-commerce has not been such a deterrent for this industry due to the aforementioned gambling connotations. With GME now offering cards, one might wonder - why would they stand to win against an incumbent like Target? The answer is that this market is big enough for many competitors and GME will offer some advantages for many consumers (smaller store, closer location, etc). Most importantly, GME’s partnership with Professional Sports Authenticator (‘PSA’) is a big differentiator. PSA is widely known to be the most reputable grading service (they grade the condition of the cards) as evidenced by having the highest after-market sale prices by far. I find it highly unlikely that TGT or other big-box retailers might start offering similar authentication submission services, giving GME a legitimate claim to having a superior trading card offering.

I note that GME is also buying PSA-graded trading cards, following the footsteps of the typical card shop business model. The idea is that GME would purchase these cards at roughly 50% to 65% of market value (I based this on recorded video experiences of such interactions) and then try to sell those cards at market price through their wide distribution channels.

I realize that it can be difficult to shake off the image of GME as just being a dying physical game store and to view it as a rising national chain of local card shops. I estimate that the company will generate around $900 million in collectibles revenue this year. If we ignore the rest of the business, then the stock is currently trading at around 12x collectibles revenues. Net of cash, that multiple drops to around 6x collectibles revenues. I expect this segment to earn a gross margin of at least 35% based on CEO Cohen’s comments about trading cards being a “higher margin business.” That would place the stock at around 17x long term earnings net of cash. Given the high growth and high quality of the trading card business, I would expect this to trade at 30x earnings at the very least. That would place this business at around $7.7 billion in value, implying 20% potential upside. There may be additional upside catalysts from memes and the bitcoin treasury strategy, but I have not accounted for those in this target.

It is worth noting that CEO Cohen is minimally compensated in spite of having an ownership stake of around 8.4%. This appears to be a management team that is fully committed to driving shareholder value.

I again am aware of the meme reputation that the stock has earned through the years, but this trading card transformation appears to be woefully ignored by the broader market.

GME Stock Risks

GME stock is likely to remain volatile for obvious reasons. It is possible that its transition to the trading card market does not go as smoothly as projected. It is possible that the company decides to invest too aggressively into Bitcoin (BTC-USD) just prior to a cryptocurrency downturn. It is also possible that the rest of the business besides collectibles deteriorates even faster than expected. The main risk here is arguably that first one listed, plain volatility, as investors should not view the stock’s surprisingly stable price action in the past year as being a predictor of future performance.

GME Stock Conclusion

GME has taken advantage of its meme origins to fix its balance sheet and create long term sustainability. The company’s recent push into the trading card market might have been glossed over due to the meme reputation, but investors might be underappreciating the high quality of that business model. I see a clear case for undervaluation just from this transition to the trading card market alone. I am upgrading the stock to a buy rating. The age of memes is over. But the age of trading cards is just beginning.

data.title: Alexandria Real Estate: A Massive Day
data.description:
Today we were asked about our opinion on Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. (NYSE:ARE). This was from a long time dividend/income investor at BAD BEAT Investing. We had been pretty sour on this name for about 2 years straight. We have not looked at the company or the stock this year, but now, we are warming up to the name after the massive drawdown after looking into the name on the member's behalf.

We will not go into detail about the past, but basically, ever since the Fed rate hiking campaign began, the company and stock have taken it on the chin. There are company-specific nuances, but those have all been covered in depth by colleagues here at Seeking Alpha. This space has been tricky, but very profitable for trading. We were short real estate investment trusts (or REITs) in 2022-2023. We started to warm up to some select names in fall 2024.

After a lot of damage in shares, we believe ARE shares are now a Buy for income, and frankly, we think there is potential to see shares retake the $100 mark as early as the end of this year. Folks, we like what we see here, and it comes just ahead of an absolutely massive day for the company. Let us discuss.

Premier property locations

For our many readers who may not be familiar, ARE operates as a REIT that owns and leases out offices for the medical, pharmaceutical, and biopharmaceutical space. So, on the one hand, it is a commercial lessor, and that has come with its own unique challenges. Commercial real estate, offices, etc., have been tough. But, the clients it works with cannot work from home, they need such spaces. The company owns property in some of the best markets in the county, including Boston, New York City San Francisco, San Diego, Seattle, among others. Let us talk recent performance here.

Headline performance

First, we think the metrics we discuss here are what you should watch for when Q2 is reported this season (due out early next week on 7/21). In the most recent quarter, the company brough in revenues that were actually up 4% from a year ago. Further, adjusted EBITDA was up 5%. That is strong.

Now, the key metric of course? That is funds from operations, or FFO. Well folks, in Q1 adjusted FFO was $2.30. That was actually a beat versus consensus on both the top and bottom line. Management did indicate that performance was impacted in the short run by costs of non-core asset sales to help with development and the company's redevelopment pipeline. There were also costs with the company's ground lease at the Alexandria Technology Square campus.

That said, while this is a short-term impact, and may impact future quarters (keep an eye out for commentary in Q2 here), this should be a long-term value creator.

Increasing rental revenue from the mega-campuses

In terms of the company's rental revenues, about 3/4 ARE's annual rental revenue comes the huge campuses it operates management thinks even more of the company's rental income will come from these mega-campuses over time. They want long-term leases and tenants that share a collaborative space. But it is all about that cash flow. Any landlord will tell you. Property value goes up and down, but vacancies are horrible as there are costs associated with them plus lack of rental income. A double whammy if you will. A

RE has over half of its annual rental revenue from investment grade and publicly traded large cap tenants. That is a plus, and we would love to see it higher.

99.9% collection rate

The other item landlords have to worry about? Non-payers. ARE has 99.9% of rent being collected. That is a plus. Further, adjusted EBITDA margins is quite strong in the space. In fact in Q1 it was 71%, among the highest it has been in 6 years. We think EBITDA margin can track near 70% the rest of the year because leasing volume cis strong. This was the fifth consecutive quarter over a million square feet leased, and we expect when Q2 is reported on July 21st we will see the sixth consecutive quarter at this threshold across the near 750 tenant relationships they have.

Other items to monitor in Q2, occupancy is the biggest

There are other trends we will monitor in Q2. And one of them is how much rents go up at lease renewal. Higher rents, along with rent escalators in leases, is the way to go. For ARE, there are positive trends here. Rental rate growth for lease renewals is pushing 20%. That is strong. Lease terms are absolutely vital for REITs. In looking deep into the portfolio, we find that lease terms are pushing 10 years on average. That means good long-term occupancy.

However, speaking of occupancy, it is not all sunshine and rainbows here folks. Occupancy has been a real issue for this REIT. It is a problem. Typically we like to see REITs in the high 90% range for occupancy. As of the end of Q1, occupancy at the end of the quarter was just 91.7%, a drop of 2.9% from the sequential quarter. For share prices to really get going with a confidence boost from the Street, we need this number to track toward the mid-90% range.

Now, the occupancy in Q1 was not exactly a surprise given Moderna (MRNA) leaving a submarket, as well as other expirations that were disclosed publicly. But that mid-90% occupancy is currently not within sight. In fact, management reduced the midpoint of occupancy guidance for the year end 2025 occupancy by 70 basis points from 92.4% to 91.7%. Most of this was due to lower re-leasing.

Softening net operating income per same office

Ok, so far, mixed news and a mixed outlook. Making matters worse, one of the key metrics we like to follow, net operating income for each property, was also down versus last year. The same office net operating income was down 3.1%. However, if we back out the big lease expirations that impacted occupancy that we talked about in the prior section, same office net operating income results would have been flat. On a cash basis, though, it would be up 9%, which is a quiet positive that is often overlooked. And yet, because of the occupancy issue and despite the strength on cash basis we saw same office growth expectations reduced by 70 basis points overall. It was also lowered 20 basis points on a cash basis.

A benefit to all REITs

So, why start to warm up to the stock now, despite what looks like a lot of work that needs to still be done? Well, compared to the trend the company was in just a few quarters ago, things in our opinion are better. We are also coming into a lower rate environment. The Fed may not lower rates in July, but we think September is on the table as well as December, and in 2026 a new Fed Chair is likely to align more with President Trump's desire for even lower rates. This will help all REITs with refinancing. Do not expect rental rates to go down, just mortgage and refinancing. A huge benefit to REITs.

Further, the company at its investor day told us that it plans to have big-time cost savings in general and administrative expenses. There will be a number of cost savings initiatives put into place this year, and so far, general and admin expenses are down some 30%. That is positive. The company expects some $49 million total savings in 2025 on this front versus last year.

Shareholder returns and balance sheet

Another reason we are getting bullish here is the return to shareholders. Folks, after the massive selloff in shares, we think the risk justifies the reward. The dividend of $1.32 quarterly translates to a 6.75% yield here at $78 per share. That is juicy income. But the company is also buying back shares. The company executed $208 million in purchases completed in Q1, with $242 million remaining under the plan authorized by the Board of Directors.

Further, this shareholder return activity comes with a pretty respectable balance sheet folks. According to the CFO, "corporate credit ratings rank in the top 10% of all publicly traded U.S. REITs. Indeed, the balance sheet is in great shape." We agree.

Reduced leverage

Leverage is always an issue to be mindful of. At the end of Q1 leverage was 5.9X on a net debt to adjusted EBITDA basis. In the world of REITs this is not horribly high, but is elevated. The company seeks to take that down to 5.2X over the next 2 quarters. Keep an eye on this leverage when Q2 is reported next week. And, we should point out that we really like the liquidity of over $5.0 billion.

One thing we did now know, and the CFO stated on the last earnings call, was that it has "the longest debt maturity profile among all S&P 500 REITs with the average remaining debt term of 12.2 years." We would have thought this was among the best, but not the best. That is bullish to us.

Looking ahead

As we look ahead, given what the company was seeing for occupancy, we were not surprised about the down guide for FFO for the year, despite the Q1 FFO beat. The company reduced guidance for adjusted FFO per share by $0.07 to a midpoint of $9.26. The range is now $9.16-$9.36. While a reduction is never welcomed, this was not all that horrific given the action in the stock itself.

That said, the company still sees its estimate for five-year growth in adjusted FFO per share at 27%. For Q2, we are looking very closely at the occupancy number. For Q2 adjusted FFO, given a strong market following April's route, a still humming economy, recognizing the long leasing periods, and taking into account expense reduction, we are looking for above consensus adjusted FFO of $2.30-$2.35.

We expect the annual guide to be maintained. All things considered, we believe Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. shares are a Buy for income here, and at each $3 decline from $78, provided the outlook is maintained.

data.title: Sibanye Stillwater Is More Than A Gold Miner, And I'm Long Because Of It
data.description:
Thesis

Sibanye Stillwater Limited (NYSE: NYSE:SBSW) used to be mainly a South African gold‑mining outfit, but it has turned itself into a global company that also produces lots of platinum‑group metals and is moving into battery minerals like lithium. It now runs mines and processing plants in South Africa, the United States, and Europe, and that spread makes it a player in the shift toward cleaner energy. My point is that this makeover, built around rich U.S. platinum deposits, in‑house smelting and refining, and newly revived gold and lithium projects, should let Sibanye make much bigger profits as platinum prices rise above its production costs and as U.S. tax credits boost its margins even more. Because the company has slimmed down and focuses on metals that are in tight supply, the recent jump in platinum prices is proof that the new strategy is working and that investors haven’t fully caught on to how much cash the business can now generate.

Sibanye Stillwater Turns Platinum Margins Positive in the U.S.

I get the sense that people used to think of Sibanye Stillwater mainly as a South African gold miner, though the firm likes to remind everyone that it also owns mines in other countries and produces several metals. The problem was platinum: for about two years the market price of platinum stayed below what it cost Sibanye to dig it up, so the company’s U.S. platinum mines looked like money‑losers even though they sit on great ore. Now platinum has climbed to roughly $1,453 an ounce and that new price is finally high enough for Sibanye to sell platinum for more than it costs to produce, giving the company a profit cushion. The margin isn’t huge in dollar terms, but I'd argue, compared with the recent past it’s a big, game‑changing improvement.

The recent breakout in platinum prices helps, but that’s not the main reason the company’s future looks brighter. What really matters is that Sibanye’s bosses finally tightened up how they run their U.S. mines. They put the old Stillwater West mine on temporary suspension last year, open since 1986, because it was no longer worth the trouble, showing they’ll put profits ahead of nostalgia. Instead, they’re pouring their efforts into two other Montana mines: Stillwater East, a newer, richer deposit that’s been built out since 2017, and East Boulder, which has been producing metal since 2002. These two sites churn out higher‑quality ore and cost less per ounce to run. The underground rock itself hasn’t changed, it still holds about 19 million ounces of confirmed precious metals and another 79 million ounces that are likely there, enough to keep mining for decades. What’s new, and crucial, is that the company is willing to stop digging any tonnage that loses money.

That move looks even smarter once you factor in government tax breaks. Thanks to the Inflation Reduction Act, the company will get about $90 million in tax credits for 2024 and another $30 million for 2025 because it produces key minerals inside the United States. Those credits push its total cost to mine and process platinum down to roughly $1,320–$1,360 per ounce. With platinum selling for around $1,453 an ounce, Sibanye now makes about $86 profit on every ounce it pulls out of the ground, about a 6% margin. Since its U.S. mines produced 425,842 ounces last year, that works out to more than $36 million in extra operating profit, even before any future efficiency gains or favorable exchange‑rate moves. The workforce is lean, just 911 people, so any small boost in productivity will add quickly to earnings. Plus, because the company processes and refines all that ore at its own smelter in Columbus, Ohio, the money stays in the U.S., something Washington, especially President Trump, likes as it tries to shore up domestic supply chains.

Yet, while the North American mines have swung from liability to asset, Sibanye refuses to let any single commodity dictate its fate. It has a gold project in South Africa called Burnstone that it put on hold during the downturn in platinum‑group metals, even after spending $280 million to develop it and planning for it to run 20 years at roughly 120,000 ounces a year. Management says they paused the project not because the gold is low‑quality or too expensive to mine, but because it seemed smarter to save cash when markets were roiling. Now that the company’s finances are stronger, they feel comfortable restarting Burnstone, which shows they’ve raised the bar for what counts as a worthwhile investment. Money isn’t as cheap or easy to get as it used to be, so any new gold mine has to be very attractive, and Burnstone finally meets that higher standard. Helping the case is the recent surge in gold prices: as of July 17, 2025, spot gold has climbed to around $3,334 per ounce, up more than 26% year-over-year, giving Sibanye fresh confidence that reviving the project will pay off.

Sibanye Stillwater’s Gold Revival Shifts the Earnings Power Base

I have to say that Sibanye’s growing optimism makes sense. In the last half of 2024, the company’s long‑running South African gold mines made about R 3.6 billion (rand), roughly $206 million, in operating profit, up 216% even though they produced 8% less gold. Naturally, most of that jump came from higher prices: the average selling price at the time climbed 26% to $2,560 an ounce, and, as we all know, mid‑2025 prices are staying very strong. All things considered, for the first time since 2017, South African gold became the company’s biggest moneymaker, passing its South African platinum‑group‑metal (PGM) division. That’s important because the U.S. side of the business still relies heavily on palladium, a metal hurt by oversupply and the move away from gas‑powered cars. So the stronger gold business, along with this rise in platinum, not only boosts overall income but also helps protect the company if demand for palladium keeps sliding.

Sibanye doesn’t rely only on digging new metal out of the ground. The company also scoops up about $32 million by stripping metals from junked catalytic converters in the U.S., and it pulls in another $34 million by wringing value out of old mine waste in Australia. True, those figures seem small compared with its big mines in Montana or South Africa, but these side businesses cost very little to run and score well on environmental goals. Because of that, investors who worry about the downsides of traditional mining like seeing profits come from what would otherwise be trash. Plus, I suspect the steady cash they generate helps smooth out the company’s overall earnings and protects it whenever a mine faces geological problems or political trouble.

Stronger cash generation has, predictably, beefed up the balance sheet. By the end of 2024 Sibanye was sitting on R 45.7 billion, about $2.4 billion, in spare liquidity, easily enough to kill off the $675 million bond coming due in 2026. Adjusted EBITDA for the year hit $715 million, the third straight half‑year of solid, if unspectacular, returns. The 2023‑24 belt‑tightening, lower U.S. capex and trimmed overhead, finally showed up as real free cash flow, just as rivals struggle with rising costs.

I’m willing to bet that the most emblematic transaction was a $500 million streaming deal with Franco‑Nevada in December 2024. Sibanye traded away less than 2% of its future gold and platinum output. That was for cash up front. And in one stroke, cut its net leverage from about 1.3x to roughly 0.7x. The swap did three things: it raised money without diluting shareholders, it left almost all of the upside if metal prices jump, and it gave Sibanye the freedom to buy back stock in a slump or pour cash into Finnish lithium and tailings projects if things heat up.

SBSW’s Operating Pressures, Forward Growth Outlook, and Equity Valuation

By the end of last year, Sibanye’s biggest headache was its U.S. platinum‑and‑palladium division. Palladium prices had stayed weak for so long that sales and asset values took a big hit. Yes, platinum prices perked up late in the year, but, at the time, that didn’t help much: on average, the metals mix Sibanye sold from its U.S. mines went for just $988 an ounce, down 21%. That was often less than it cost to produce, so profit margins were squeezed hard and the U.S. unit flipped from moneymaker to money loser. Because of all this, Sibanye’s operating profit (EBITDA) fell from $1.1 billion in 2023 to $715 million in 2024, a 37% drop, and after taxes and special charges the company ended up with a $311 million net loss.

Even though metal prices were swinging around, company leaders realized they had bigger long‑term problems to fix right away. So, as I pointed out, they shut down the Stillwater West mine, let go of 636 employees, and shifted their focus to two other Montana mines that produce higher‑quality ore. I’m inclined to think that move should improve overall grades, but it also means the company will haul out about 200,000 fewer ounces of metal in 2025.

Granted, Sibanye just wrote off $479 million on its U.S. platinum‑and‑palladium assets. I get the sense that the company's admitting its old forecasts for palladium demand no longer work now that hybrids and electric cars are taking over. But the company’s problems aren’t only in the United States or only about palladium. In 2024, total sales stayed basically flat at about $6.1 billion because weak prices hit both its South African and U.S. mines. South Africa’s mix of four precious metals sold for 16% less, and the U.S. mix fell 21%, as mentioned earlier. I gather that having mines in two countries and selling different metals helped a little, but not enough to offset the broad slide in prices.

On the tax front, things went from good to bad. In 2023 Sibanye actually got a $131 million tax credit, but in 2024 it had to pay $81 million in taxes. That swing squeezed the company’s cash even more. Because of that, the board decided not to pay a year‑end dividend. Giving up the dividend props up the balance sheet, sure, but it ticks off investors who expect mining stocks to spit out cash in good times. Looks to me like management would rather patch the ship and keep their options open than toss money overboard right now. That makes sense, since U.S. operations have very little wiggle room on costs and the South African mines still depend on an unreliable power grid and a volatile local currency.

Nevertheless, while the stock is up +160% YTD, I actually suspect there's more room to go higher.

At the time of my analysis, at $8.59, SBSW is 27% below what some models project it’s really worth ($11.79) and almost two‑thirds under an implied future value of $25.40.

Although the forward P/E for 2025 looks high at 16.74, it drops to 8.93 in 2026, implying earnings could double within 18 months. That upbeat view assumes Sibanye cuts costs, focuses on higher‑quality ore even if it mines less of it, and benefits from stronger platinum and gold prices, all of which would send more money straight to the bottom line.

Other valuation yardsticks tell the same story. If you compare Sibanye’s total value to its expected earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization (EV/EBITDA), the stock trades at about 5.7x, roughly one‑third cheaper than the industry average. Look at earnings after depreciation and amortization (EV/EBIT) and it’s about 8.1x, or a 40% discount to similar companies. The price‑to‑sales ratio is just 0.93, showing the market is skeptical.

Yet the company’s operating cash flow is projected to jump roughly 48%, about six times faster than the sector.

Because of this, a forward price‑to‑cash‑flow of 3.62 hints at real upside if those cash flows appear.

Final Verdict

Rating: Buy. I think the shares still trade at a steep discount to fair value even after a big year‑to‑date run, and the combination of rising platinum and gold prices, leaner U.S. operations, and a stronger balance sheet should let earnings rebound sharply. Granted, costs remain tight and palladium demand is a headwind, but the tax credits, streaming cash, and higher‑grade focus give the company enough cushion that I’m comfortable calling this a buy rather than just a hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lennox Q2 Preview: Price Increase And Volume Decline Ahead, Initiate With Hold
data.description:
Lennox International Inc. (NYSE:LII) manufactures Furnaces, air conditioners, heat pumps and refrigeration systems, operating through Home Comfort Solutions and Building Climate Solutions segments in both residential and commercial markets. Lennox planned to adjust its product price effective early in Q2 FY25 to mitigate its tariff costs. I think the company is well positioned to capture the HVAC market growth; however, I think the stock price is slightly overvalued; therefore, I am initiating with a Hold rating with a fair value of $573 per share.

As detailed in the table below, Lennox has experienced robust growth in both topline and bottom line in recent years, driven by strong pricing growth in both Home Comfort Solutions and Building Climate Solutions segments. In addition, the management has done a great job to manage its overhead costs, expanding its overall net income margins with 26.2% year-over-year growth in FY24.

As depicted in the slide below, Lennox has worked on its factory automation and distribution profitability initiatives to reduce costs and accelerate its business growth over the past few years. As noted in its call, 90% of its cost structures are based in the North America, which has minimized its risks to the U.S.-China trade disputes and tariffs. Notably, only 5% of their business is exposed to the impact from U.S.-China tariff duties.

However, due to the tariffs on aluminum and steel, Lennox anticipates a 9% cost inflation for FY25, as noted in its recent earnings call. The management announced the price increases in early Q2 to mitigate the tariff duties, anticipating a 7% price increase, up from previous 1% guidance. While I believe the overall HVAC market will raise price because of tariffs, it is likely that Lennox cannot fully offset its cost pressure in the near term.

Lennox released its Q1 FY25 result on April 23, delivering 2% organic revenue growth with 4% decline in volume, 1% growth from price and 5% increase from product mix, as detailed in the chart below:

Due to the tariff costs and tariff impacts and factory inefficiencies, its segment profits declined by 7% during the quarter. During the earnings call, the management noted that the new factories for Building Climate Solutions business experienced production inefficiencies due to ramp-up and regulatory transitions.

My key takeaway from this quarter was the 9% volume decline in its commercial business caused by weak demands and channel destocking activities. The management anticipated half of the volume decline was due to destocking and end-customers pre-purchases in anticipation of upcoming tariffs. It is likely that the volume pressure from pre-buy activities will remain in the upcoming quarter.

Lennox is scheduled to release its Q2 FY25 result on July 23 before the market open. The company is guiding for 2% revenue growth and $22.25 to $23.50 in EPS for the full year, as detailed in the slide below:

Due to the pre-buy activities in FY24, the company anticipates the total volume will decline by 4% for FY25. Indeed, Lennox is facing substantial cost inflation coupled with volume pressure for this fiscal year.

As the Trump’s administration recently announced a 50% tariff on copper effective August 1, I think the management might adjust its cost inflation estimates in the upcoming quarter. The rising raw material costs will become a significant headwind for the company’s margin expansion for FY25, in my opinion.

For FY25, I anticipate Lennox will deliver 2% revenue growth, in line with its guidance. I project its operating margin will decline by 20bps in FY25, assuming its price adjustment in Q2 cannot fully offset the tariff costs on steel, aluminum and copper. From FY26 onwards, I project the revenue will normalize to 7% of growth assuming 3% pricing growth and 4% volume growth, which aligns with the average growth rate over the past six years. I project 20bps annual margin expansion from FY26 onwards, driven by 10bps from pricing increases and 10bps from reduction in SG&A costs. I calculate the normalized operating expenses will grow by 6.8% annually, leading to a 19.9% operating margin by FY34.

It's worth noting that Lennox maintains a robust balance sheet with a net debt leverage of 0.8x. In May 2025, the company announced a $1 billion increase to its existing stock repurchase authorization. As a result, I anticipate the total number of shares outstanding will decline by 1% annually.

The DCF can be summarized as follows:

I calculate FCFF as follows:

I estimate the WACC to be 9.9% assuming risk free 4.2%; beta 1.01; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $21 billion; debt $1.1 billion; tax rate 20%. I set the terminal growth to be 5% assuming 3% price growth and 2% volume growth. Discounting all the future FCFF and adjusting net debt, the fair value is calculated to be $573 per share, according to my DCF.

Key Risks

While Lennox has limited tariff exposure in China, the company has huge exposures to U.S.-Mexico tariff risks. As shown in the table below, Mexico accounts for more than 30% of total property, plant and equipment. If the U.S. government imposes any tariffs to Mexico, Lennox will face additional cost pressures on top of the sector tariffs on steel, aluminum and copper.

While around 75% of Lennox’s businesses are tied to the replacement market, a quarter of its revenues are tied to the new construction market, which is very sensitive to the interest rate and macro environment. The U.S. housing market has experienced sluggish growth in recent months due to high mortgage rates, and it is unlikely for the company to generate meaningful volume growth from its new construction market.

Lastly, more than 30% of its employees are represented by unions, according to its 10K. The union structures will limit the company’s capabilities to structure its workforce and reallocate its manufacturing facilities in the future.

Conclusion

While Lennox International Inc. faces near-term challenges in volume growth, destocking activities and cost inflations, I think the company has the pricing power to gradually pass these additional costs to its end-customers over time. I am initiating with a Hold rating with a fair value of $573 per LII share.

data.title: FDIS: Consumer Discretionary Dashboard For July
data.description:
This article reports industry metrics, aiming at a top-down analysis of the consumer discretionary sector. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLY) and Fidelity MSCI Consumer Discretionary Index ETF (NYSEARCA:FDIS), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each subsector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for retailing in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in retail companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.A floor of -100 is set for VS and QS when the calculation goes below this value. It may happen when metrics in a subsector are very bad.

data.title: Standard Chartered: Taking Stock After The Big 2025 Rally
data.description:
The market has come around to Standard Chartered PLC (OTCPK:SCBFF), a UK-domiciled regional banking group that derives most of its profits from Asia, since my prior bullish calls (see Standard Chartered: Stay The Course and Standard Chartered: High-Quality Global Bank Priced At A Deep Discount). So much so that the group, despite its outsized exposure to tariffs (via the trade finance and Asia-centric nature of its business), has been among the year’s best-performing banking names.

Coming off a solid Q1 and some very positive signals into its upcoming Q2 report as well, Standard Chartered looks to be full steam ahead. That said, there have also been some significant new developments recently, Namely, a $2.7bn lawsuit relating to a Malaysian investment fund scandal, as well as interest rate shifts and flows relating to its wealth management unit, all of which warrant a closer look.

On the $2.7bn 1MDB Bombshell

Standard Chartered was rocked with a surprise civil lawsuit earlier this month. The allegation, by the liquidators of Malaysian state investment fund 1MDB, relates to compliance failures that allowed over $2.7bn to be laundered through the bank during the 2009-2013 period. In response, Standard Chartered stated that they “consider these claims are without merit” and “will vigorously defend against the claims”. The group’s press release added that they “reported the transaction activities of these companies” and “shut their accounts in early 2013”.

At first glance, a $2.7bn liability seems significant enough to warrant investor attention. But there are important caveats here. For one, even if successful, litigation will drag on for a while, so any one-off fine is likely to be stretched over many years. Secondly, the 1MDB issue isn’t new – Standard Chartered has already come under regulatory scrutiny from the Monetary Authority of Singapore (Singapore’s financial regulator or “MAS”) back in 2016, with the investigation finding no “pervasive control weaknesses or wilful misconduct”.

Now, even in a worst-case scenario, where Standard Chartered does incur the full $2.7bn fine, it’s worth noting that the group has more than enough cushion to absorb this hit. For context, the group reported tier 1 capital of $42.6bn in Q1 2025, of which common equity tier-1 capital (i.e., CET1 or the highest quality capital immediately available for loss absorption) contributed $35.1bn.

This capital base is only going to grow in the coming years as well, helped by $4-$5bn of yearly profits, so I see no liquidity or solvency risk at this point. As for Standard Chartered’s $8bn distribution target, the ample capital buffer at hand means its current +10% total shareholder return yield (dividends + buybacks) is likely to be unaffected as well.

On Rates and Cross-Border Flows

Declining interest rates, as we are seeing throughout Asia, are a big negative for bank margins, and Standard Chartered is no different. Note, for instance, the continued decline in net interest income in recent quarters. And with tariffs knocking a few points off Asian growth as well, Q1 2025 estimates saw the group’s currency-weighted average rate assumption further declining by a cumulative ~141bps through fiscal year 2026.

In Standard Chartered’s case, however, there are unique mitigating factors. For one, while the previously published sensitivity at the H1 2024 interim outlined a $650m per 100bp rate move, today’s rate sensitivity is likely a lot lower thanks to “materially increased” hedging in recent quarters.

The group’s leadership in an increasingly active Hong Kong-China corridor (largely thanks to record-low interest rates in mainland China) has also been useful in attracting deposits out of China and cushioning the margin impact. From here, all signs point to Chinese policy rates falling further as growth slows and tariffs kick in; in this scenario, Standard Chartered is well placed to benefit.

On the Wealth Management Opportunity

As resilient as the group has proven to be through near-term tariff headwinds (recall management citing a “positive start” to Q2 on the last earnings call), the key reason to own Standard Chartered remains its longer-term wealth management opportunity. For context, the goal here, centered on serving a growing affluent class in Asia, is $200bn of net new money and double-digit “wealth solutions” annualized growth through 2029.

So far, the group, having heavily ramped up hiring in recent years, is making good on its promises. Mainly by onboarding new-to-bank affluent customers, but also by up-tiering existing clientele, all of whom are ‘sticky’; as management describes, these are “very long-term savers” and “very long-term investors” that come with low retention costs. As things stand, Standard Chartered is seeing a very significant acceleration in net new affluent money (+22% YoY) from its shift, driving a $389bn base of affluent assets in Q1.

Heading into Q2 reporting later this month, it’s encouraging that the group hasn’t seen any adverse impact from “Liberation Day” in April. Per management, things have actually been positive, as “by all the metrics into the early part of Q2, we've seen new clients, new money and income performing with the momentum that we saw in Q1.” The key here is Standard Chartered’s one-stop shop offering, which includes more defensive products and should thus continue to mitigate outflows if we do see more macro uncertainty going forward.

Summing Up

In all, with Standard Chartered already tracking at the upper end of its mid-term targets and sustaining that momentum into early 2025, the setup looks good into earnings later this month. In the meantime, valuations, while higher following this year’s big rally, still leave room for a lot more upside at the current ~20% discount to forward book. From here, look for further progress on return on capital (over 14% return on tangible equity target) and return of capital (over 10% total distribution yield via buybacks + dividends) to catalyze the next leg higher.
