data.title: TSMC Q2: AI Growth Is Booming, Yet I'm Holding Off
data.description:
Since my last Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited aka TSMC (NYSE:TSM) analysis, the stock gas gained 7% in price.

TSMC just posted Q2 results, sending the stock toward record highs. I personally deem the stock a little overvalued right now, based on its historical sentiment and the maturity of the AI capex cycle. However, many momentum and growth investors see TSMC stock more positively, given a robust future growth outlook and record Q2 earnings showing strong operating efficiencies and a durable strategy to secure shareholder returns.

Q2 Earnings Show Continued Operating Strengths

In Q2 2025, in New Taiwan dollars, the company delivered record revenue from 38.6% year-over-year growth, predominantly driven by AI and high-performance computing chips. Net income grew by 60.7% year-over-year, with earnings per share up by the same amount. Even stronger growth was exhibited as the company demonstrated strong internal efficiency with a 61.7% year-over-year growth in operating income. Operating expenses were just 9.1% of revenue, down from 10.5% year-over-year, leading to a high operating margin of 49.6%. We can expect further margin strength from the company in 2026, as it is considering price hikes for advanced nodes as there is currently tight capacity and high customer dependency; these are truly effects of a world-class moat.

It's not surprising that the company is experiencing so much growth as the AI and high-performance computing opportunity is now in full bloom. High-performance computing (which includes AI semiconductors) is the dominant revenue share, at about 60%, up from 52% a year ago. Smartphones are approximately 27%, down from 33% a year ago. These two segments are the lion's share of the business' cash flows.

North American revenue is a stark 75% of TSMC's total, compared to just 9% for China, which is down sharply year-over-year. This shows, structurally, a major competitive advantage that America and the free world have over China right now. It will be very difficult for China to compete with TSMC using domestic companies, and TSMC is evidently adhering politically to the liberal West. This alliance of TSMC and Western countries (dominantly America) does create a strong level of growth security, as there is lower risk of containment than if the company's revenues were oriented more toward China. That said, with easing China-U.S. relations appearing likely to continue in the medium term, we could see TSMC's Chinese revenue exposure increase again as the country "opens up."

Management provided guidance that was certainly robust. Revenue growth was forecast at around 30% year-over-year, up from ~25%, due to continued expansion of the AI opportunity. Q3 guidance pointed to 38% year-over-year revenue growth. For investors considering buying TSMC stock as a long-term investment, or for those who already own it as such, it's key to notice the evolving structural tailwinds currently fueling sentiment. Management sees AI-related revenues doubling in 2025 and then continuing at a 40-45% compound annual growth rate over the next five years. This AI-related growth trajectory provides significant resilience to short-term consumer electronics softness.

Also, despite very high capital expenditures at present to support infrastructure for burgeoning advanced semiconductor manufacturing demand, the company is still able to deliver substantial free cash flow. Moreover, as the company's moat becomes even more expansive, it is likely that its free cash flow margin will increase moderately further (albeit in cycles), as it will be able to spend less on infrastructure and still increase its prices while maintaining market leadership.

Value Investors Know Now Is Not The Best Time To Buy TSMC Stock

I consider TSMC stock to be much better suited to growth and momentum investors right now, as even though the company still looks "cheap" on paper, based on the reality of market sentiment, it's slightly overextended in price for reliable medium-term alpha to accrue.

The stock is trading well above the 50-week moving average in price, and the 14-week RSI has now essentially breached 70, indicating it is overbought based on a medium-term horizon.

Also, looking at the stock's historical sentiment related to the Wall Street consensus, there's little indication that the stock will reliably outperform the consensus outlook, as it never really has done in the last five years. Indeed, the professional market is, understandably, bullish on TSMC stock. But when a whole market is bullish on a company, it's difficult for it to outperform expectations. Given the reality of hedge funds, retail investors, and most capital allocators using the Wall Street consensus as a reference point for forward sentiment, I can't see TSMC stock delivering outsized near-term alpha. There is, in simple words, a sentiment anchor to this stock that is hard for management to overcome. This is compounded by the maturity of the AI opportunity, likely contracting valuation multiples moderately (rather than expanding them) as top-line growth slows.

My current 12-month price target for TSMC stock is constituted as follows: The current non-GAAP P/E ratio is 30, and given substantially moderating growth in Fiscal 2026 compared to Fiscal 2025, I forecast that this will contract to about 25 in 12 months in a base case. I also forecast about $10.50 in normalized earnings per share for the middle of 2026, indicating a stock price of about $260 in 12 months. The current price is $245, so the upside is marginal, and my rating is Hold as a result.

Certain Operational Risks Also Constrain Sentiment For Now

Currently, TSMC's Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate ("CoWoS") packaging capacity is fully tapped, creating a supply chokepoint. Although the company is rapidly ramping packaging, delays in the company's expansions in Taiwan and Arizona could constrain revenues.

Additionally, broad Trump-era tariffs across steel, aluminum, and (potentially) semiconductors of up to 50% will almost certainly contribute to margin pressures for TSMC. However, because these tariff-related profitability pressures will be affecting almost all companies, sentiment could stay more sustainable than if they were company-specific.

I think the Taiwan geopolitical risk is manageable right now if the Trump administration treats China with respect and diplomatic competition rather than heightening a trade war. Understandably, the U.S. administration wants to create global security after the devastating wars in Ukraine, Israel, Gaza, and Iran. However, deterrence is different from provocation, and Trump's actions now could have long-lasting implications on the markets, particularly on TSMC (as it is central to geopolitical agendas).

Conclusion: Hold

TSMC stock is undoubtedly one of the investments on the market that connects strongly with investors looking for a resilient moat and monopoly-like free cash flow generation. Q2 results were impressive, and there is every indication that sentiment will remain steady for the stock in the near term, but it is unlikely to expand rapidly and deliver ultra-high alpha.

Put simply, TSM stock is most likely moderately overvalued, given the moderation in top-line growth coming in the following financial years. I reiterate my Hold rating, despite such strong Q2 results.
